name: Minamoto no Raiko
description: Minamoto no Yorimitsu, also known as Minamoto no Raiko, was a celebrated historical figure and legendary hero from Japan's Heian period. Born into the distinguished Minamoto clan, Yorimitsu rose to prominence as a powerful warrior and loyal servant of the Imperial Court. He is best known for his extraordinary feats of bravery and his legendary battles against supernatural creatures, demons, and monsters that plagued the land. Raiko's stories are deeply embedded in Japanese folklore, representing the triumph of order and courage over chaos and evil. He served as a symbol of justice and protection, his name often invoked to ward off evil spirits. He is often depicted as a strong and resourceful leader, accompanied by his loyal band of retainers, the Shitenno (Four Heavenly Kings), who aided him in his perilous quests. Raiko's exploits include slaying the monstrous spider Tsuchigumo, defeating the oni Shuten-doji, and confronting the earth spider giant. These tales, passed down through generations, have solidified Raiko's place as one of Japan's most revered and enduring heroes, embodying the values of loyalty, bravery, and the relentless pursuit of justice. His legends have been adapted into various forms of art, literature, and theater, ensuring his continued relevance in Japanese culture.
subtitle: Demon Slayer of Heian Japan

keyMyths.title: The Slaying of Shuten-doji
keyMyths.description: Minamoto no Raiko and his retainers infiltrate Shuten-doji's mountain fortress, using poisoned sake to weaken the oni before slaying him. This act brought peace to the region terrorized by the demon.

keyMyths.title: The Defeat of Tsuchigumo
keyMyths.description: Raiko, suffering from a mysterious illness, discovered the spider demon Tsuchigumo was the cause and bravely fought and defeated it. The spider's blood was said to stain the ground.

keyMyths.title: The Subjugation of the Earth Spider
keyMyths.description: Raiko, after an illness, discovered an earth spider disguised as a yamabushi. He cut it down, revealing its true form and ending its reign of terror.

sources.author: Helen Craig McCullough
sources.work: The Taiheiki: A Chronicle of Medieval Japan
sources.description: Provides historical context and recounts some of Raiko's exploits.
